title: Two Sum
description:
Given an array of integers nums and an integer target, return indices of the two numbers such that they add up to target.

You may assume that each input would have exactly one solution, and you may not use the same element twice.

You can return the answer in any order.

starter.python:
from typing import List

def twoSum(nums: List[int], target: int) -> List[int]:
    # Your code here
    pass

test_cases.description: Basic case with target at indices 0 and 1

hints: Think about how to check if the complement (target - current number) exists in the array.
Consider using a hash map to store previously seen numbers and their indices.
Remember that you can't use the same element twice, so check the index when you find a complement.
solution: Use a hash map to store the complement (target - current number) as key and its index as value. For each number, check if it exists in the hash map. If it does, return the indices. This gives O(n) time complexity.

title: Valid Parentheses
description:
Given a string s containing just the characters '(', ')', '{', '}', '[' and ']', determine if the input string is valid.

An input string is valid if:

1. Open brackets must be closed by the same type of brackets.
2. Open brackets must be closed in the correct order.
3. Every close bracket has a corresponding open bracket of the same type.

examples.explanation: All brackets are closed correctly and in order.
Closing bracket ] doesn't match opening bracket (.

hints: Consider using a stack data structure to keep track of opening brackets.
When you encounter an opening bracket, push it onto the stack.
When you encounter a closing bracket, check if it matches the top of the stack.
solution: Use a stack to keep track of opening brackets. Push opening brackets onto the stack, and when encountering closing brackets, check if they match the top of the stack. If they match, pop from the stack. The string is valid if the stack is empty at the end.

title: Binary Search
description:
Given an array of integers nums which is sorted in ascending order, and an integer target, write a function to search target in nums. If target exists, then return its index. Otherwise, return -1.

You must write an algorithm with O(log n) runtime complexity.

starter.python:
from typing import List

def search(nums: List[int], target: int) -> int:
    # Your code here
    pass

examples.explanation: 9 exists in nums and its index is 4
2 does not exist in nums so return -1

hints: Use binary search algorithm with O(log n) complexity.
Initialize two pointers: left at 0 and right at len(nums) - 1.
Calculate the middle index and compare nums[mid] with target.
solution: Use binary search: initialize left and right pointers, calculate mid index, compare nums[mid] with target, and adjust pointers based on comparison. This achieves O(log n) time complexity.
constraints: All the integers in nums are unique
nums is sorted in ascending order
